MA Supplemental benefits are benefits for items and services related to the health of the enrollee.
heading: 1600.2.58 Medicare Advantage (MA) Supplemental Benefits

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count the following military allowances as earned income: Base pay BAH BAQ BAS Sea-duty pay Career sea pay VHA Ration ( BAS ) Hazard Pay received prior to deployment to a combat zone or received due to deployment to an area not designated as a combat zone.

Exception : See Combat Pay .

If two payments are received in one month because a holiday or weekend changes the mailing cycle, count the payments for the months for which they are intended.

Exclude CMA s as a reimbursement.

Exclude amounts withheld from paychecks for education programs.
heading: 1600.2.59 Military Pay

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Many payments made to Native Americans under federal law are totally excluded income. Some payments are partially excluded. Click here for a list of public laws and the treatment of funds.
heading: 1600.2.60 Native American Funds Excluded by Federal Statute

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude $2000 per year from tribal dividend payments.

Exceptions : Exclude all tribal dividend money deposited into a child’s trust. Count casino profit payments as unearned income in the month received. Do not apply the $2000 exclusion.
heading: 1600.2.61 Native American Tribal Dividends

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count as unearned income any direct money payments without a work requirement that can be construed to be a gain or benefit.
heading: 1600.2.62 Other Unearned Source

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count payments as unearned income.
heading: 1600.2.63 Pensions

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude the amount of money necessary to fulfill SSI’s PASS . This includes money deposited into a special PASS account.
heading: 1600.2.64 Plan for Achieving Self-Support (PASS)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count as unearned self-employment income .
heading: 1600.2.65 Property Sold on Contract

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income property settlements paid as cash in more than one payment.

Exclude property settlements paid as cash in one payment as a lump sum (nonrecurring).
heading: 1600.2.66 Property Settlement

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude.

A temporary (up to 24-month) assistance program that helps families transitioning to work. It provides support services for employment such as help paying car repairs, work clothes, work supplies, 100% child care subsidy and a food benefit.
heading: 1600.2.67 Reach Ahead

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income even if the assistance is given in the form of a vendor payment. Also count the payment as unearned even if the program requires the applicant/recipient to work without pay in order to be eligible.

A temporary (four month) financial assistance program for families who have a plan to become self-sufficient within four months.
heading: 1600.2.68 Reach First

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count as unearned income even if the assistance is given in the form of a vendor payment . Also count the payment as unearned even if the program requires the applicant/recipient to work without pay in order to be eligible.
heading: 1600.2.69 Reach Up

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude this payment for completing a parenting education program.
heading: 16002.70 Reach Up Parenting Incentive Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count as unearned income.
heading: 1600.2.71 Refugee Cash Assistance (RCA)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude money that is withheld from the household’s income to repay a prior overpayment from that source. Also exclude money the household received, but voluntarily or involuntarily returned to repay a prior overpayment from that income source. Exception : See Repayment due to Intentional Program Violation (IPV) .
heading: 1600.2.72 Recoupment

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude.
heading: 1600.2.73 Refunds from Rent or Utility Deposits

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude reimbursements for past or future expenses as long as the reimbursements meet all of the following criteria: They do not exceed actual expenses. If the reimbursement exceeds actual expenses, count the excess as unearned income. Do not consider a reimbursement to exceed actual expenses unless the provider or the household says it exceeds actual expenses. They do not result in a gain or benefit to the household. They are identified and used for expenses other than normal living expenses (such as rent, mortgage, personal clothing, or food eaten at home). Count payments as income if they are not identified for a specific purpose or are not used for that purpose.

If a reimbursement covers multiple expenses, each expense does not have to be separately identified, as long as none of the money covers normal living expenses.

The following are examples of excluded reimbursements: Reimbursements or flat allowances for job or training related expenses, such as per diem, uniforms, and transportation to or from the job or training site. Exclude reimbursements for these expenses that are over and above basic wages. Reimbursements for the out-of-pocket expenses volunteers incur in the course of their work. Medical or child care reimbursements. Medicare Advantage (MA) Supplemental Benefits Reach Ahead work support payments The payments are reimbursements for past or future work expenses incurred by participating families.
heading: 1600.2.74 Reimbursements

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count as earned self-employment income. Exception : If a third party is employed to manage the property, it is unearned self-employment income .
heading: 1600.2.75 Rental Property Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count money as unearned income when it is withheld from federal, state, or local means-tested programs to recoup an overpayment caused by the household’s intentional failure to comply with program requirements.

If the amount withheld is not due to intentional program violation, exclude it as a regular recoupment. See Recoupment .

Federal, state or local means-tested programs include Reach Up, General Assistance, State Supplementary Assistance, SSI, and veteran pensions.

Note : To determine if recoupment from SSI is an IPV, ask if it is due to being criminally charged with an IPV. If it is, then it is considered an IPV. Verify IPV status for SSI only when the household states it was due to IPV.
heading: 1600.2.76 Repayment Due to Intentional Program Violation (IPV)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
A payment received by one person, the representative payee, for the needs of another person, the beneficiary. When the representative payee and the beneficiary are in the same 3SVT household, count the payment as unearned income to the household.

When the representative payee and the beneficiary are not in the same 3SVT household, treat the representative payment as follows: Count as unearned income to the beneficiary any part of the payment that the payee gives to, or uses for the needs of the beneficiary. Count as unearned income to the payee any amount that is not given to or used for the needs of the beneficiary.

When a representative payment is made to a payee that is intended for more than one beneficiary, and the beneficiaries are both 3SVT household members and non-members exclude only the identifiable portion of the payment intended and used for the care and maintenance of the non-member.

If a non-member’s portion cannot be readily identified, divide the payment evenly among all beneficiaries. Exclude the non-member’s prorata share or the amount actually used for the non-member, whichever is less.
heading: 1600.2.77 Representative Payment

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count retirement payments as unearned income.
heading: 1600.2.78 Retirement Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude if there is a verifiable agreement to repay the loan.

Exclude income from a loan against the home with no repayment required until the borrower dies, or the home is sold.

Count as unearned income if there is no repayment agreement.
heading: 1600.2.79 Reverse Mortgage

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count royalty payments as unearned income.

A payment to an owner for the use of property, especially patents, copyrighted works, franchises or natural resources.
heading: 1600.2.80 Royalties

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count the gross income from self-employment, including the total gain from the sale of any capital goods or equipment related to the business, excluding the allowable costs of doing business. See Self-Employment .
heading: 1600.2.81 Self-Employment Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude wages received from the Title V Senior Community Service Employment Program of the Older American’s Act. An employment-based job training program for older Americans that provides training for low-income, unemployed seniors.

Vermont SCSEP includes: Experience Works Vermont Associates for Training and Development, Inc. National Able Network Vermont Department of Aging and Independent Living Visiting Nurses Association
heading: 1600.2.82 Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude payments and allowances from SeniorCorps, without exception.

Note : Green Mountain Grandparents is a SeniorCorps program.
heading: 1600.2.83 SeniorCorps Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude as a lump sum (nonrecurring), if paid in a single payment.

Count as unearned income if paid in more than one payment.

The compensation that an employer provides to an employee who has been laid off, whose job has been eliminated, who through mutual agreement has decided to leave the company, or who has parted ways with the company for other reasons.
heading: 1600.2.84 Severance Pay

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as earned income when the person gets it while employed.

Count as unearned income when the person getting it is no longer an employee and is paid out in more than one check over a period of time.

Exclude the sick pay as a lump sum (nonrecurring) when paid in one payment and the person getting it is no longer an employee.

When co-workers donate their sick leave time, count the payments the same as if they were the person’s own sick pay.
heading: 1600.2.85 Sick Pay

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income. Count the gross amount even if Social Security tax or a Medicare premium is withheld.

Exclude Social Security retroactive payments as lump sum (nonrecurring) .

Exclude Social Security benefits withheld to repay a prior overpayment regardless if due to intentional program violation or not . See Recoupment .
heading: 1600.2.86 Social Security Benefits

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income for a person when: their spouse is a LTC recipient, the spouse is not a member of the 3SVT household, and spousal allocation is required in the LTC budget.

Exclude spousal allocation when the spouse is in the 3SVT household.
heading: 1600.2.87 Spousal Allocation

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Counts as unearned income.

See Strikers .
heading: 1600.2.88 Strike Benefits and Picket Pay

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
A scholarship, grant, loan, veteran’s educational benefit and the like awarded to a household member enrolled at a: Recognized post-secondary education institution School for the handicapped Vocational education program Vocational or technical school Program that provides for obtaining a secondary school diploma or the equivalent

Exclude all student financial assistance received under programs in Title IV of the Higher Education Act or under Bureau of Indian Affairs student assistance programs. These include but are not limited to: Basic Education Opportunity Grants (BEOG or PELL Grants) Presidential Access Scholarships (Super Pell Grants) SEOG SSIG Federal Family Education Loan Program (Formally GSL) Supplemental Loans for Students PLUS Loans for parents Robert T. Stafford Student Loans Direct Federal Loans to students in institutions of higher education Perkins Loans (formerly NDSL) Federal Work Study Funds ( Note : Not all Federal work study funds come under Title IV of the Higher Education Act) Trio Grants Upward Bound Student Support Services Robert E. McNair Post-Baccalaureate Achievement Robert C. Byrd Honors Scholarship Program CAMP for students whose families are engaged in migrant and seasonal farm work HEP National Early Intervention Scholarship and Partnership Program

Exclude all loans and scholarships received under Titles III, VII and VIII of the Public Service Health Act. These include, but are not limited to: National Health Service Corps NURSE Corps Scholarship Program Scholarships for Disadvantaged Students Loans for Disadvantaged Students Health Professions Student Loans Nursing Student Loans Primary Care Loans NIH Undergraduate Scholarship NIH Clinical Research Loan Repayment Indian Health Service Scholarships Indian Health Service Loan Repayment Native Hawaiian Scholarship Program

Count as unearned income other scholarships, educational grants, deferred payment loans for education and veterans’ educational benefits that do not have a work requirement attached to them. Count as earned income if they have a work requirement attached to them. Exclude amounts used for: Tuition Mandatory fees, including rental or purchase of any equipment, material and supplies related to the course of study pursued Books Transportation Miscellaneous personal expenses, other than normal living expenses, incidental to attending school such as, professional journals, typing of papers, membership in professional associations, etc. Dependent care ( Note : any amount excluded from income cannot be claimed as part of the dependent care expense. See Dependent Care .) Origination fees and insurance premiums on educational loans

Student income provided for living expenses is counted as unearned income unless there is a work requirement attached to its receipt. Count as earned income if it has a work requirement attached. Treat student income provided for living expenses this way whether or not the student receives the payment or it is paid directly to the provider.

Count the following grants as unearned income if used for living expenses. Exclude any portion earmarked for allowable expenses (tuition, books, transportation, etc.): VSA Grant Summit Grant VT State Grant
heading: 1600.2.89 Student Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income.

SSI benefits are sometimes paid to a Representative Payee for the SSI recipient. See Representative Payment .

Exclude retroactive SSI payments as a lump sum (nonrecurring) .

Also, exclude retroactive or underpayment SSI payments deposited directly into a dedicated account when an SSI recipient is under the age of 18 and must have a representative payee. If the person is eligible for retroactive SSI, the representative payee must set up a “dedicated” account in a financial institution, and must use this money for allowable expenses of the child. Do not count any disbursements from this account as income.

Exclude SSI benefits withheld to repay a prior overpayment, unless it was due to intentional program violation (IPV ). Verify IPV status only when the household states it was due to IPV. See Recoupment .
heading: 1600.2.90 Supplemental Security Income (SSI)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude earnings received as a temporary census worker. Send to AOPs as this must be tracked.
heading: 1600.2.91 Temporary Census Worker

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as earned income.

Note : Basic Wage Rate applies when employers are unable to estimate tips.
heading: 1600.2.92 Tips

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count training allowances from vocational and rehabilitative programs recognized by federal, state or local governments as earned income, as long as they are not a reimbursement .
heading: 1600.2.93 Training Allowances

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count Tribal TANF payments as unearned income.

Exclude any retroactive payments as a lump sum (nonrecurring) .
heading: 1600.2.94 Tribal TANF Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income money withdrawn from an inaccessible trust that is excluded as a resource. Send the trust to AOPs for review.

Exception : Exclude when the withdrawal is a reimbursement .

If the trust is accessible to the household and therefore counted as a resource, exclude as income any money withdrawn from the trust.

If the household has the option of either receiving dividends as income or reinvesting them in the trust, count them as unearned income in the month they are credited to the trust.
heading: 1600.2.95 Trust Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count unemployment insurance benefits as unearned income.

Exclude retroactive unemployment insurance payments as a lump-sum (nonrecurring) . Exclude unemployment insurance benefits withheld to repay a prior overpayment. See Recoupment .

UCB is paid by the Vermont Department of Labor to a person whose job has ended due to lay-off, firing without good reason, or quitting with good reason.
heading: 1600.2.96 Unemployment Compensation Benefits (UCB)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude as reimbursements from the Uniform Relocation Assistance and Real Property Acquisition Policy Act of 1970 (Public Law 91-646, Section 216).
heading: 1600.2.97 Uniform Relocation Assistance and Real Property Acquisition Policy Act

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude Universal Basic Income/Guaranteed Basic Income payments that provide money regardless of employment status to support basic needs if: Excluded by Reach Up, Paid with solely private or a combination of private and public funds. Amount must be the same amount excluded for Reach Up.

Example : Spectrum Direct Cash Transfer Pilot Program. For any other programs, send an inquiry to AOPs.
heading: 1600.2.98 Universal Basic Income/Guaranteed Basic Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as earned income if it is received before employment ends.

Count as unearned income when received after employment ends, when paid in more than one payment.

Exclude as a lump sum (nonrecurring) when it is received in one lump sum after employment ends.
heading: 1600.2.99 Vacation Pay

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude the following vendor payments: Payments made from their own funds by a person outside the household or by an organization to a third party for a household’s expense. Payments made by a person sharing housing with the 3SVT household to a third party for household’s shelter expense. Rent or mortgage payments made to the landlord or lender by the Department of HUD or by state or local housing authorities. Payments by a government agency/program to a third party on behalf of the household to pay the household’s medical or child care expenses. Payments through the Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program. Housing assistance when a household lives in temporary housing. Payments converted to a direct cash payment when approved by a federally authorized demonstration project.

E xceptions: See Legally Obligated Money and General Assistance .

See Determining Monthly Deductions - Disallowed Expenses on how to handle deductions.

Exclude General Assistance or Reach Up vendor payments for the housing and transportation of Migrant Worker and Seasonal Farmworker households while workers are in the job stream. See Migrants and Seasonal Farmworkers .
heading: 1600.2.100 Vendor Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Exclude the utility allowance portion of VSHA Section 8 Homeownership program payments. See HUD Utility Payments . Count as unearned income any remaining portion paid to the customer.

Exclude VSHA Section 8 Homeownership program payments that are paid directly to the lender. See Vendor Payments .
heading: 1600.2.101 Vermont State Housing Authority (VSHA) Section 8 Homeownership Program

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income payments made by the Department of Veterans Affairs to veterans or dependents of veterans. This includes: The Monthly Housing Allowance provided to veteran students for housing costs under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, Chapter 31 Disability payments based on exposure to Agent Orange, Pension, Disability compensation, Aid and Attendance Pension , and Household Allowance .

See Agent Orange Settlement Fund if the customer is receiving payments for Agent Orange exposure but it is not paid by the VA.
heading: 1600.2.102 Veteran’s Benefits

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: See AmeriCorps VISTA .
heading: 1600.2.103 VISTA Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as earned income training allowances from vocational and rehabilitative programs recognized by Federal, State or local governments, such as work incentive programs, to the extent they are not a reimbursement .

Example : Career Training Offset.
heading: 1600.2.104 Vocational Rehabilitation (HireAbility VT) Training Allowance

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Count all wages and salaries as earned income.
heading: 1600.2.105 Wages

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Exclude payments for work-related expenses as reimbursements.
heading: 1600.2.106 Welfare-to-Work Payments

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as unearned income, excluding any fees withheld. See Costs Withheld from Unearned Income .

Workers’ compensation is a benefit paid to an employee who is injured on a job covered by the program. Workers’ compensation insurance is mandatory for all Vermont employers.
heading: 1600.2.107 Workers’ Compensation

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Count as earned income WIOA on-the-job training, including Job Corps and YouthBuild earnings. These earnings include monies paid by WIOA and monies paid by the employer. WIOA sets the definition of these programs.

Exception : Exclude WIOA on-the-job training earnings of household members under age 19 who are under the Parental Control of an adult household member. Apply this exclusion regardless of school attendance or enrollment.

Exclude all payments for living allowances, education, or other purposes that participants get for participating in WIOA programs.

Note : The Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act was formerly known as JTPA .
heading: 1600.2.108 Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: All questionable information must be verified. Do not verify income that is excluded, unless the exclusion is questionable.
heading: 1600.3.1 Requirement to Verify All Questionable Information

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Before initial approval, verify all gross countable income.

Exception : Allow 3SquaresVT in a SNAP households to self-declare income, unless questionable.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.
heading: 1600.3.2 Application

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Verify changes in gross non-excluded income.
heading: 16003.3 Interim Report

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Benefit increase : Verify changes in gross countable income.

Benefit decrease : Verify changes at recertification.

Exception : Allow 3SquaresVT in a SNAP households to self-declare income, unless questionable.
heading: 1600.3.4 Unscheduled Changes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Verify gross countable income.

Exception : Allow 3SquaresVT in a SNAP households to self-declare income, unless questionable.
heading: 1600.3.5 Recertification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body: Base the income on the household’s written statement if good faith efforts to obtain the verification failed because the source failed to cooperate with the household and the ES.
heading: 1600.3.6 Verification Not Available

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

body:
Release # 25-6

Effective Date: 12/15/2025

Legal Base Food and Nutrition Act of 2008, as amended. 7 CFR § 273.9 Vermont Elderly Simplified Application Project, Approved Waivers.
heading: 1600.5 Last Update

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1600 Income

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
New information in the manual is highlighted and noted with a . See Manual Updates and for details.

Section with expandable text are noted with a throughout the manual.
heading: 1700 Deductions

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Households may be eligible for 6 deductions from gross income. These deductions are: Legally Obligated Child Support Dependent Care Earned Income Deduction Medical Expenses Shelter Expenses Standard Deduction

The household does not have to actually make a payment to get a deduction, but the household must be responsible for the expense. Exception : Child support payments must be paid to get a deduction for that expense.

Expenses that are covered by excluded reimbursements or excluded vendor payments are not allowed.

This section defines allowable expenses. See Determining Income and Deductions for instructions on budgeting the expense.
heading: 1700.1.1 Types of Household Deductions

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Allow a child support deduction when the support is Legally Obligated and is: Paid by a household member for a child who is not part of the 3SVT household; or Paid by a household member to an agency or person outside of the household if the payments are not returned to the household.
heading: 1700.2.1 Child Support Deduction

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow the amount actually paid by the household member, including any past-due (arrearage) amounts.

Child support may include but is not limited to: Monthly child support Legal fees Any past-due (arrearage) amounts Health Insurance payments
heading: 1700.2.2 Child Support Amount

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Do not allow payments if the following occurs: The payment is made through an IRS offset payment. The payment is defined as alimony, spousal support, or property settlement. The payment is not court-ordered (voluntary).

Do not allow the portion of a processing fee that is not passed along to the parent/guardian.
heading: 1700.2.3 Expense Not Allowed

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Verify the legal obligation, the legally obligated amount, the actual amount paid, and that it is paid to a non-household member before a deduction is allowed.
heading: 1700.2.4 Verification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If verification is not submitted prior to the end of the 30-day processing period, process the application without the deduction.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.
heading: 1700.2.4.1 Application

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new payment or change is reported and verification is not submitted by the requested due date, process the interim report without the deduction.
heading: 1700.2.4.2 Interim Report

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If a new payment is reported and verification is not submitted by the requested due date, process without the deduction.

If a change in payment is reported and benefits will: Increase, verify the new amount, if verification is not provided do not make a change; use the previously verified amount. Decrease, process the change and verify at recertification.
heading: 1700.2.4.3 Unscheduled Changes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new obligation is reported and verification is not submitted prior to the end of processing time period, process the recertification without the deduction. If a change in payment is reported, verify the change. If verification is not submitted by the due date, process without the payment.
heading: 1700.2.4.4 Recertification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Allow the actual cost of dependent care expenses, for a child under 18, including transportation . Examples of dependent care providers are: Private arrangements with persons not in the household Before or after school programs, such as the Boys and Girls Club, YMCA, YWCA, etc. Note: Elementary, middle and high school tuition, fees and transportation are not allowable dependent care expenses. Summer camp fees Preschool programs
heading: 1700.3.1 Dependent Care Provider Examples

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow child or dependent care costs only if the care is needed to permit all parents or guardians to: Look for work Accept a job or continue working, regardless of whether the income is counted or excluded Comply with Work Requirements Attend training or education to prepare the person for work

All parents or guardians in a household must need the care by meeting one of the above. Exception : See Special Dependent Care Situations .

Allow the following costs: The unreimbursed billed expense for children or another dependent member(s); and/or Transportation costs to and from program sites. Use the actual cost of public transportation or .585 cents per mile.
heading: 1700.3.2 Expense Allowed

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow if: Applied for or in receipt of unemployment compensation; or Complying with the Reach Up requirements.

Note : Reach Up case manager must verify dependent care is required for compliance.
heading: 1700.3.2.1 Look for Work

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Do not allow a child care expense: If the expense is for elementary, middle or high school; If another parent or guardian is in the household and able to provide the care; If another household member provides the care; If the care is paid for by some in-kind benefit, such as food; or For the portion of expense covered by an excluded reimbursement or vendor payment, such as Child Care Services. Note : This includes any portion covered by excluded educational income.

If a parent or guardian report that they are unable to provide care for the child and require dependent care, see Special Dependent Care Situations .
heading: 1700.3.3 Expense Not Allowed

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow dependent care costs if parents or guardians claim: They are Disabled and unable to care for the child, A person who meets one or more of the following: Receives disability or blindness benefits from any of these programs: Social Security, SSI , or MABD . A person meets this even when the full benefit is withheld or deferred due to recoupment. Receives a federally or state administered SSI supplement based on disability or blindness, or section 212(a) of PL 93-66. Receives a disability retirement benefit from a government agency for a disability considered permanent by SSA disability considered permanent by SSA . Any of the following conditions: Permanent loss of use of both hands, both feet, or one hand and one foot. Amputation of leg at hip. Amputation of leg or foot because of diabetes or peripheral vascular diseases. Total deafness, not correctable by surgery or hearing aid. Statutory blindness, unless caused by cataracts or detached retina. IQ of 59 or less, established after the person becomes 16 years old. Spinal cord or nerve root lesions resulting in paraplegia or quadriplegia. Multiple sclerosis with damage to the nervous system caused by scattered areas of inflammation. The inflammation recurs and has progressed to varied interference with the function of the nervous system, including severe muscle weakness, paralysis, and vision and speech defects. Muscular dystrophy with irreversible wasting of the muscles, impairing the ability to use the arms or legs. Impaired renal function caused by chronic renal disease, resulting in severely reduced function which may require dialysis or kidney transplant. Amputation of a limb of a person at least 55 years old. Is a veteran the VA considers totally (100%) disabled or permanently housebound or in need of regular aid and attendance. Is a veteran’s surviving spouse who the VA considers: in need of regular aid and attendance, or permanently housebound, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA. Is a veteran’s surviving child who the VA considers: incapable of self-support, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA They are unfit to care for the child, or They claim domestic violence where the child is at risk being left alone with the 2nd parent.

Verification of the exception is mandatory. Send to AOPs for review.
heading: 1700.3.4.1 Unable to Provide Care

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow dependent care costs when parents or guardians are temporarily out of work and have a return-to-work date.

Temporarily out of work can include but is not limited to: Family or medical leave (FMLA) Seasonal or short term lay off Short term disability

Verification is not required, unless questionable.
heading: 1700.3.4.2 Future Return to Work Date

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Verification is not required unless the information is questionable. Take the following actions only if verification is requested.
heading: 1700.3.5 Verification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If verification is not submitted prior to the end of the 30-day processing period, process the application without the expense.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.
heading: 1700.3.5.1 Application

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new expense or change is reported and verification is not provided by the requested due date, process the interim report without the deduction.
heading: 1700.3.5.2 Interim Report

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If a new expense is reported and verification is not submitted by the requested due date, process without the deduction.

If a change in expense is reported and benefits will: Increase - if verification is not provided by the requested due date, do not make a change; use the previously verified amount. Decrease - process the change and verify at recertification.
heading: 1700.3.5.3 Unscheduled Changes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new expense or a change in expense is reported and verification is not submitted by requested due date, process the recertification without the expense.
heading: 1700.3.5.4 Recertification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Subtract 20% of Gross Earned Income .

Do not allow the 20% gross earned income deduction when determining an overpayment amount if the household failed to report earned income correctly or timely. See Overpayment for more details.
heading: 1700.4 Earned Income Deduction

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow the standard medical expense or actual medical expenses for: Senior or Disabled household members; A person who meets one or more of the following: Receives disability or blindness benefits from any of these programs: Social Security, SSI , or MABD . A person meets this even when the full benefit is withheld or deferred due to recoupment. Receives a federally or state administered SSI supplement based on disability or blindness, or section 212(a) of PL 93-66. Receives a disability retirement benefit from a government agency for a disability considered permanent by SSA disability considered permanent by SSA . Any of the following conditions: Permanent loss of use of both hands, both feet, or one hand and one foot. Amputation of leg at hip. Amputation of leg or foot because of diabetes or peripheral vascular diseases. Total deafness, not correctable by surgery or hearing aid. Statutory blindness, unless caused by cataracts or detached retina. IQ of 59 or less, established after the person becomes 16 years old. Spinal cord or nerve root lesions resulting in paraplegia or quadriplegia. Multiple sclerosis with damage to the nervous system caused by scattered areas of inflammation. The inflammation recurs and has progressed to varied interference with the function of the nervous system, including severe muscle weakness, paralysis, and vision and speech defects. Muscular dystrophy with irreversible wasting of the muscles, impairing the ability to use the arms or legs. Impaired renal function caused by chronic renal disease, resulting in severely reduced function which may require dialysis or kidney transplant. Amputation of a limb of a person at least 55 years old. Is a veteran the VA considers totally (100%) disabled or permanently housebound or in need of regular aid and attendance. Is a veteran’s surviving spouse who the VA considers: in need of regular aid and attendance, or permanently housebound, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA. Is a veteran’s surviving child who the VA considers: incapable of self-support, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA A person who meets one or more of the following: Receives disability or blindness benefits from any of these programs: Social Security, SSI , or MABD . A person meets this even when the full benefit is withheld or deferred due to recoupment. Receives a federally or state administered SSI supplement based on disability or blindness, or section 212(a) of PL 93-66. Receives a disability retirement benefit from a government agency for a disability considered permanent by SSA disability considered permanent by SSA . Any of the following conditions: Permanent loss of use of both hands, both feet, or one hand and one foot. Amputation of leg at hip. Amputation of leg or foot because of diabetes or peripheral vascular diseases. Total deafness, not correctable by surgery or hearing aid. Statutory blindness, unless caused by cataracts or detached retina. IQ of 59 or less, established after the person becomes 16 years old. Spinal cord or nerve root lesions resulting in paraplegia or quadriplegia. Multiple sclerosis with damage to the nervous system caused by scattered areas of inflammation. The inflammation recurs and has progressed to varied interference with the function of the nervous system, including severe muscle weakness, paralysis, and vision and speech defects. Muscular dystrophy with irreversible wasting of the muscles, impairing the ability to use the arms or legs. Impaired renal function caused by chronic renal disease, resulting in severely reduced function which may require dialysis or kidney transplant. Amputation of a limb of a person at least 55 years old. Is a veteran the VA considers totally (100%) disabled or permanently housebound or in need of regular aid and attendance. Is a veteran’s surviving spouse who the VA considers: in need of regular aid and attendance, or permanently housebound, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA. Is a veteran’s surviving child who the VA considers: incapable of self-support, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA Household members receiving emergency SSI benefits based on presumptive eligibility; and/or A non-household member if the following conditions are met: The person meets the senior/disabled definition; The person was in the household immediately prior to being placed in an institution (hospital, nursing home, etc.) or death of the member; The person cannot establish 3SVT eligibility on his/her own; and The household is paying the medical expenses of the person.

On-going and one-time expenses are allowed but have different budgeting procedures. See Determining Income and Deductions .

Medical expenses start the month the person turns 60 or the month the person is determined Disabled . A person who meets one or more of the following: Receives disability or blindness benefits from any of these programs: Social Security, SSI , or MABD . A person meets this even when the full benefit is withheld or deferred due to recoupment. Receives a federally or state administered SSI supplement based on disability or blindness, or section 212(a) of PL 93-66. Receives a disability retirement benefit from a government agency for a disability considered permanent by SSA disability considered permanent by SSA . Any of the following conditions: Permanent loss of use of both hands, both feet, or one hand and one foot. Amputation of leg at hip. Amputation of leg or foot because of diabetes or peripheral vascular diseases. Total deafness, not correctable by surgery or hearing aid. Statutory blindness, unless caused by cataracts or detached retina. IQ of 59 or less, established after the person becomes 16 years old. Spinal cord or nerve root lesions resulting in paraplegia or quadriplegia. Multiple sclerosis with damage to the nervous system caused by scattered areas of inflammation. The inflammation recurs and has progressed to varied interference with the function of the nervous system, including severe muscle weakness, paralysis, and vision and speech defects. Muscular dystrophy with irreversible wasting of the muscles, impairing the ability to use the arms or legs. Impaired renal function caused by chronic renal disease, resulting in severely reduced function which may require dialysis or kidney transplant. Amputation of a limb of a person at least 55 years old. Is a veteran the VA considers totally (100%) disabled or permanently housebound or in need of regular aid and attendance. Is a veteran’s surviving spouse who the VA considers: in need of regular aid and attendance, or permanently housebound, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA. Is a veteran’s surviving child who the VA considers: incapable of self-support, or approved for benefits because of the veteran’s death and has a disability considered permanent by SSA

Explore medical expenses during the interview process for households with at least one senior or disabled member.
heading: 1700.5.1 Eligible Household Members

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Allow the following medical expenses: Attendant, homemaker, home health aide, child care, or housekeeper necessary due to age, infirmity or illness; Note : Treat attendant care costs that qualify either as a medical or dependent care expense as a medical expense. Subtract the 1-person benefit amount from the cost if the household furnishes the majority of the attendant's meals. Allow the benefit that is in effect at the time of approval. Ambulance services; Cost sharing or spend down expenses incurred by Medicaid members; Note : This includes the Choices for Care/Long Term Care patient share. Information is found in ELIG/D/LONG. This is considered verified upon receipt. Credit card or loan principal payments if the medical expense was paid with the card/loan; Note : Do not allow interest payments as a medical expense Dental care, including denture costs; Eye glasses and contact lenses prescribed by an ophthalmologist or optometrist; Hearing aid costs; Hospitalization or outpatient treatment; Insurance premiums for health and hospitalization costs; Note : Only allow the premium of the senior or disabled household member. If there is not specific information on separation of the premium amount, allow a prorated amount; Lifeline/MedicAlert costs if prescribed by a Qualified Health Professional See Examples ; To determine if a person is licensed in Vermont, go to Vermont Secretary of State Licensee Look Up or Vermont Health Professionals Look Up . Examples include but are not limited to: Acupuncturists Allied Mental Health Applied Behavior Analysis Chiropractic Dental Examiners Dietitians Midwives Naturopathic Physicians Nursing Optometry Osteopathic Physician Physical Therapists Physicians Physicians Assistants Podiatrists Psychoanalyst Psychologic Examiner Respiratory Care Practitioner Substance Abuse Counselors Medical care including psychotherapy and rehabilitation services provided by a Qualified Health Professional See Examples ; To determine if a person is licensed in Vermont, go to Vermont Secretary of State Licensee Look Up or Vermont Health Professionals Look Up . Examples include but are not limited to: Acupuncturists Allied Mental Health Applied Behavior Analysis Chiropractic Dental Examiners Dietitians Midwives Naturopathic Physicians Nursing Optometry Osteopathic Physician Physical Therapists Physicians Physicians Assistants Podiatrists Psychoanalyst Psychologic Examiner Respiratory Care Practitioner Substance Abuse Counselors Medical equipment and supplies, including rental equipment when recommended by a Qualified Health Professional See Examples ; To determine if a person is licensed in Vermont, go to Vermont Secretary of State Licensee Look Up or Vermont Health Professionals Look Up . Examples include but are not limited to: Acupuncturists Allied Mental Health Applied Behavior Analysis Chiropractic Dental Examiners Dietitians Midwives Naturopathic Physicians Nursing Optometry Osteopathic Physician Physical Therapists Physicians Physicians Assistants Podiatrists Psychoanalyst Psychologic Examiner Respiratory Care Practitioner Substance Abuse Counselors Medicare/Medi-Gap premiums; Note : Do not enter the Medicare premium on ACCESS if the Payer is “Self” on the MEDI panel. Membership fees for primary care services; Nursing care; Nursing home costs; Over-the-counter medication, including insulin and vitamins, when recommended by a Qualified Health Professional (see Examples ), including the cost of postage for mail order over-the-counter medication; Note : CBD which contains no more than .3 percent THC is an allowable medical expense. Send full product information to AOPs for pricing. To determine if a person is licensed in Vermont, go to Vermont Secretary of State Licensee Look Up or Vermont Health Professionals Look Up . Examples include but are not limited to: Acupuncturists Allied Mental Health Applied Behavior Analysis Chiropractic Dental Examiners Dietitians Midwives Naturopathic Physicians Nursing Optometry Osteopathic Physician Physical Therapists Physicians Physicians Assistants Podiatrists Psychoanalyst Psychologic Examiner Respiratory Care Practitioner Substance Abuse Counselors Prescription drugs when prescribed by a Qualified Health Professional (see Examples ), including the cost of postage for mail-order prescription drugs; To determine if a person is licensed in Vermont, go to Vermont Secretary of State Licensee Look Up or Vermont Health Professionals Look Up . Examples include but are not limited to: Acupuncturists Allied Mental Health Applied Behavior Analysis Chiropractic Dental Examiners Dietitians Midwives Naturopathic Physicians Nursing Optometry Osteopathic Physician Physical Therapists Physicians Physicians Assistants Podiatrists Psychoanalyst Psychologic Examiner Respiratory Care Practitioner Substance Abuse Counselors Prosthetic costs; Service animal costs, including purchase, maintenance, veterinary care, food, etc. The animal must be trained to perform some function that the disabled/senior member cannot readily perform or to help compensate for a physical condition. The trainer does not need to have any special credentials and can be the person claiming the expense as long as the animal is trained to do a specific function the owner cannot do for themselves. To determine if the animal is a service animal ask; What service is the animal providing that meets the needs of the disabled/senior individual, and What training did the animal receive to be able to provide the service. Transportation and lodging to obtain medical care, prescription drugs, over the counter medication, etc. Note : For transportation, allow the actual cost if paid to a non- household member. If a household member drives, allow .585 cents per mile. Confirm the customer used their vehicle to drive, to allow mileage.
heading: 1700.5.2 Allowable Medical Expenses

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Do not allow the following medical expenses: Expenses paid or that will be paid by another source (insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, etc.); Expenses that are paid to a collection agency; Items that can be purchased with 3SVT benefits such as: Water, Vinegar; Insurance premiums for health and accident policies such as: those payable in lump sum settlements for death or dismemberment, income replacement policies regardless of payment schedule, or income maintenance policies such as those that continue mortgage or loan payments; Special diets regardless if the diet is related to a medical condition. Examples Examples Ensure, juices, nutritional supplements, etc. Utility costs from the use of medical equipment; Any Schedule I controlled substance considered illegal by federal law. Marijuana MDMA (Ecstasy) CBD (Cannabidiol) which contains more than .3 percent THC.
heading: 1700.5.3 Expense Not Allowed

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
No deduction is allowed if the household’s allowed medical expenses are less than $35.01.

The standard medical expense is used if allowed medical expenses are at least $35.01 and under $191.

If the household's allowed medical expenses are greater that $191, the household may choose the standard medical expense or actual expenses.

The medical deduction is the total cost of the allowed medical expense minus $35. For example , the medical deduction is $156 when the standard medical expense is claimed ( $191 - $35).
heading: 1700.5.4 Standard Medical Expense

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Verify medical expenses (including the amount of reimbursements) before allowing a deduction.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.

Verification of other factors, such as the eligibility of the Senior or Disabled Disabled person or if services provided are allowable, is required if questionable.
heading: 1700.5.5 Verification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Verification is required for at least $35.01 in medical expenses. If the household reports more than $191 in medical expenses, verification is required for all reported medical expenses if the household chooses to have actual medical expenses deducted. If the expenses are greater than $191 and the household chooses the medical standard, and at least $35.01 is verified, give them the standard.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.
heading: 1700.5.5.1 Application

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If a household that was not receiving the medical deduction reports medical expenses that would result in a deduction, follow the verification requirements in application .

If a change of more than $25 is reported and the household wishes to: Continue with the medical standard, no verification is necessary. Continue with actual expenses, verify the new expense(s). If verification is not provided, do not allow the new expenses. Continue with the previously verified expenses.

If a change of more than $25 is reported and it results in the household choosing to switch between the medical standard and actual expenses the household must verify all reported expenses prior to receiving a deduction for actual expenses. If verification is not received, continue with the medical standard.

If a change of more than $25 is reported and benefits will decrease: If the household is receiving the medical standard and the change results in less than $35.01 in medical expenses, remove the medical standard. Verification is not required. If the household is using actual medical expenses, and the change results in Expenses less than $191 but more than $35.01 switch the household to the medical standard. Verification is not required. Continued expenses of over $191 make the change and verify at recertification.
heading: 1700.5.5.2 Interim Report

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If a household that was not receiving the medical deduction reports medical expenses that would result in a deduction, follow the verification requirements in application .

If a change of more than $25 is reported and the household wishes to: Continue with the medical standard, no verification is necessary. Continue with actual expenses, verify the new expense(s). If verification is not provided, do not allow the new expenses. Continue with the previously verified expenses.

If a change of more than $25 is reported and it results in the household choosing to switch between the medical standard and actual expenses the household must verify all reported expenses prior to receiving a deduction for actual expenses. If verification is not received, continue with the medical standard.

If a change of more than $25 is reported and benefits will decrease: If the household is receiving the medical standard and the change results in less than $35.01 in medical expenses, remove the medical standard. Verification is not required. If the household is using actual medical expenses, and the change results in Expenses less than $191 but more than $35.01 switch the household to the medical standard. Verification is not required. Continued expenses of over $191 make the change and verify at recertification.

Exception : If the change is not reported by the household, act on the change only if it is verified upon receipt, such as the Medicare Part B premium reported through BENDEX. Otherwise, do not request verification and do not act on the change.
heading: 1700.5.5.3 Unscheduled Changes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Follow the verification requirements in application .

Exception : Do not re-verify expenses for a household that is currently receiving the medical standard.
heading: 1700.5.5.4 Recertification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: CATN
heading: 1700.5.5.5 CATN

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: The household’s shelter expenses are used for the calculation of net income and benefit amount. The shelter expense is based on the BSD (see below) and the HSD (see Homeless Shelter Deduction ).
heading: 1700.6.1 Deductible Shelter Expenses

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: The shelter expense must be a continuing charge that leads to ownership of the residence or rental payments for the residence. Only allow expenses for the residence the household lives in. Exception : Expenses for an unoccupied home are allowed if the home meets the unoccupied home requirements.
heading: 1700.6.2 Basic Shelter Deduction (BSD)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow costs of current rent or mortgages including: All mortgage loan repayments, including second mortgages, home equity loans and home equity lines of credit, if the home property is used as collateral for the loan; Payments to buy a mobile home, RV or travel trailer when used as a primary dwelling; Must include a kitchen and bathroom to be allowed. Lot rent; Interest fees on mortgage/mobile home payments; Property taxes ; Home insurance; Condominium and association fees; Unreimbursed repair or replacements costs that was substantially damaged by a natural disaster, such as flood or fire; and/or Hotel cost when used as a primary dwelling.

Do not allow the following costs: A “mechanic’s lien” or similar lien Mortgage life insurance premiums; Late payment fees; Extra charges, such as garage space, pet charges, etc.; Repair or replacement costs unless they result from a natural disaster; Down payments; Charges added to the rent to recoup for past unpaid rent amounts; Portion of expense covered by an excluded reimbursement or vendor payment; Payment for a vehicle without a kitchen and bathroom, even if used as the primary dwelling; or Auto insurance for a vehicle used as a primary dwelling.
heading: 1700.6.2.1 Rent and Mortgage

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow hotel costs as shelter expense when the cost is related to a short or long-term stay and the hotel room is the customer's primary dwelling. Allowable cost related to hotel rooms are: Standard Room cost Taxes

Non-Allowable cost related to hotel rooms are: Room service charges Cost of Damages

Do not allow hotel cost as shelter expense when the cost is related to a temporary stay, such as vacations.

Allow the hotel cost for households in Emergency Housing subject to income contribution requirements. Use the number of days multiplied by the hotel rate if paying or incurring the expense.
heading: 1700.6.2.2 Hotel Cost

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Allow property taxes as shelter expenses. Allowable cost related to property taxes are: Net property tax amount listed on property tax bill (amount after any State/Fed payments).
heading: 1700.6.2.3 Property Taxes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Allow the cost of insurance on the structure itself, such as fire, flood and liability insurance, if the policy separates the costs of the structure from the contents. If the insurance policy does not separate these costs, allow the entire amount.

Allow service charges such as an added fee for monthly billing but do not allow late payment charges.

Do not allow renters insurance.
heading: 1700.6.2.4 Property Insurance

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
The HSD is a standard deduction for homeless households who incur or expect to incur allowable homeless shelter cost . The HSD is n ot determined by actual shelter cost.

Allow the HSD when: All household members are Homeless ; The household incurs or expects to incur monthly shelter, utility expenses or basic living cost ; and The household’s BSD is equal to or less than the current HSD standard.

Note: Homeless household with a PHON panel qualify for the HSD.

Do not allow the HSD when: All household members are not homeless. All household members are homeless but: are receiving free shelter; or do not claim shelter, utility, or basic shelter cost. The BSD provides the household a larger deduction.
heading: 1700.6.3 Homeless Shelter Deduction (HSD)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Shelter and utility cost incurred or expected to be incurred by the household can include basic shelter and utility costs or basic living costs (see below).
heading: 1700.6.3.1 Allowable Homeless Shelter Cost

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Basic living cost are considered shelter cost for a homeless household. These costs include but are not limited to: Doing Laundry Trash Disposal Storage Fees Phone Calls Transportation Costs Parking Fees Mail Fees Fuel or Wood for heating or cooking Equipment for camping or alternative shelter

Note: Basic living cost are not included in the calculation of the BSD. They make a homeless household eligible for the HSD.
heading: 1700.6.3.2 Basic Living Cost

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Shelter costs of an unoccupied home may be allowed if the household intends to return to the home and the members are away from the home for: Training; Employment; Illness; or Abandonment because of natural disaster or casualty loss.

Do not allow unoccupied home costs if: The home is being rented out or leased during the absence of the household members; and/or Any current residents are claiming shelter costs for the home for 3SVT or SNAP deductions in any state.

The household is allowed only one utility allowance to cover both residences. Allow the highest applicable utility allowance.
heading: 1700.6.4 Unoccupied Home

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: When separate 3SVT households share a residence, and one household gives its share of basic shelter expenses to the other who then pays them, give each household a deduction for their part of the basic shelter expenses. Shared Households
heading: 1700.6.5 Multiple Households in One Residence

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Individuals who report they are will lose their housing within 14 days are considered Homeless .

Shelter costs must be updated based on the details the household provides. Note: Dependent care transportation cost must also be updated.
heading: 1700.6.6 Imminently Homeless

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Shelter costs should be removed starting the month after they will lose housing, or earlier if they report that costs have already ended.

Households must report any changes in their situation to have shelter costs re-added to the case.

The HSD indicator on the ADDR screen should be updated to “Y” (if eligible) in the month they become homeless or imminently homeless.
heading: 1700.6.6.1 Changes Reported in Person or Over the Phone

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Attempt to call the household to gather more information.

If you’re unable to reach them, send a VCR.

If details are provided (by phone or in response to the VCR), follow the changes reported in person or other the phone process above.

If there is no response to the VCR: Remove shelter costs starting the month after they will lose housing (unless other details are available in CATN). Clarify shelter costs at the next IR or recertification. Households must report changes in their situation to have shelter costs added back. Update the HSD indicator to “Y” (if eligible) in the month they become homeless/imminently homeless.
heading: 1700.6.6.2 Changes Reported in Writing or reported to other programs.

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Assign the appropriate utility allowance based on the household’s utility obligation. The utility allowances are: Standard Utility Allowance (SUA) Basic Utility Allowance (BUA) Telephone Allowance
heading: 1700.6.7.1 Assignment of Utility Allowances

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
Heating fuel, including propane tank rental fee and wood purchases when wood is the primary heating source

Cooling (air-conditioning)

Electricity

Cooking Fuel

Water/sewage, including well and septic tank installation and maintenance

Garbage/trash collection, including fees paid at a transfer station to dispose of household trash

Telephone, including a mobile phone

Fees charged for installation of utilities

Excess heating or cooling charges
heading: 1700.6.7.2 Allowable Utility Costs

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
The SUA includes all utility costs including installation/maintenance.

Assign the SUA SUA to a household that meets one of the following criteria: Is responsible for paying any portion of the heat or air conditioning costs directly to the provider, to a landlord, or to someone with whom the household shares these expenses (including excess or flat fees for these costs); or Households with a Senior or disabled member that receive LIHEAP payments (Fuel or Home Heating Assistance); or Households that have a Senior or disabled member and receives the $21 annual Fuel Assistance Program payment ; or Receives a HUD, FmHA, or other program energy assistance payment, reimbursement or vendor payment and the household incurs out-of-pocket heating or cooling costs that exceed the amount of the payment; or Households with a Senior or disabled member who received weatherization services through the Vermont Home Weatherization Assistance Program within the past 12 months. Verification is not required unless questionable. Note: The $21 Annual Fuel Assistance Program Payment will still be issued for these households. Renters whose building was weatherized in the past 12 months qualify. Utility Allowances Allowance Amount SUA (with heat) $1,096 BUA (without heat) $311 Telephone $37

Households that meet one of these criteria are eligible for the SUA year-round.

Heating costs must be for primary heating and do not include: Space heaters Electric blankets Heat lamps Use of the cooking stove to heat

Cooling costs are for central or room air conditioners and do not include fans.
heading: 1700.6.7.3 Standard Utility Allowance (SUA)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: A $21 annual payment will be paid to 3SVT households if the household is not currently eligible for the SUA through any other criteria. The payment is made the month of application, and annually thereafter. The payment does not have to be for the current residence nor does the household have to have a current heating or cooling cost. Only households with a senior or disabled member are allowed the SUA if the household received this payment the month of application or anytime in the prior 12 months.
heading: 1700.6.7.4 $21 Annual Fuel Assistance Program Payment

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
The BUA includes all non-heating/non-cooling costs including installation/maintenance.

Assign the BUA to a household that does not qualify for the SUA but is responsible for paying any portion of two of the following expenses directly to the provider, to a landlord, or to someone with whom the household shares these expenses (including flat fees for these costs): Electricity Cooking fuel Water/sewage, including well and septic tank installation and maintenance Garbage/trash collection Telephone Utility Allowances Allowance Amount SUA (with heat) $1,096 BUA (without heat) $311 Telephone $37
heading: 1700.6.7.5 Basic Utility Allowance (BUA)

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Give the telephone allowance to households when this is their only utility expense. This includes households which share the telephone expense. Utility Allowances Allowance Amount SUA (with heat) $1,096 BUA (without heat) $311 Telephone $37
heading: 1700.6.7.6 Telephone Allowance

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Multiple households are assigned the full amount of the SUA/BUA or Telephone Allowance as long as each household qualifies for it. For example , if two households split the heating costs for the residence, each will get the full SUA.
heading: 1700.6.7.7 Multiple Households in One Residence

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: Verification is not required unless the information is questionable. Take the following actions only if verification is requested.
heading: 1700.6.8 Verification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If verification is not submitted prior to the end of the 30-day processing period, process the application without the expense.

Postpone verification for expedited service month(s), unless verification can be provided in time to meet the seven-day standard of promptness.
heading: 1700.6.8.1 Application

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new expense or change is reported and verification is not provided by the requested due date, process the interim report without the deduction.
heading: 1700.6.8.2 Interim Report

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body:
If a new expense is reported and verification is not submitted by the requested due date, process without the deduction. If a change in expense is reported and benefits will: Increase, if verification is not provided by the requested due date, do not make a change; use the previously verified amount.

Decrease, process the change and verify at recertification.
heading: 1700.6.8.3 Unscheduled Changes

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: If a new expense or a change in expense is reported and verification is not submitted by requested due date, process the recertification without the expense.
heading: 1700.6.8.4 Recertification

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions

body: All households receive a standard deduction based on the number of eligible household members. See Standard Deduction Table . Standard Deduction Household Size Amount 1 $209 2 $209 3 $209 4 $223 5 $261 6 $299 Over 6 $299
heading: 1700.7 Standard Deduction

metadata.source_authority: Vermont Department for Children and Families
metadata.title: Vermont 3SquaresVT Manual: 1700 Deductions
